Name: efficient_kernel_optimization_via_adaptive_precision
Title: Adaptive Precision Kernels: A New Paradigm for Efficient Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: Can adaptive precision kernels dynamically adjust their numerical precision based on real-time workload characteristics to optimize GPU throughput without requiring retraining or extensive manual tuning?
Related Work: Current literature focuses on static precision assignments for optimizations, which can lead to inefficiencies in GPU utilization. For example, works like 'Spectral Quantization Routing' propose fixed precision strategies based on profiling but do not incorporate dynamic adjustments based on real-time performance metrics. My proposal distinguishes itself by introducing an adaptive mechanism that alters kernel precision based on live profiling data, ensuring optimal performance across varying workloads.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demand efficient inference solutions to meet real-time performance requirements on commodity GPUs. Current approaches often rely on fixed precision settings, which can hinder GPU utilization and throughput. In this work, we propose a novel framework for adaptive precision kernels that leverage real-time performance profiling to dynamically adjust numerical precision during inference. By employing a lightweight monitoring system that tracks execution metrics such as latency, memory usage, and computational throughput, our method identifies optimal precision settings on-the-fly. We hypothesize that allowing kernels to adapt precision based on workload characteristics will yield substantial reductions in latency and increases in throughput without necessitating changes to the underlying model architecture. Our experiments will focus on common transformer architectures, with an emphasis on profiling kernel performance under varying precision strategies and reporting empirical results on wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second metrics. This work promises to bridge the gap between theoretical optimization strategies and practical, deployable solutions for efficient LLM inference.
Experiments: Implement a baseline model using fixed precision kernels and measure performance metrics (latency, throughput).
Develop an adaptive precision kernel that monitors real-time performance metrics during inference and adjusts precision settings dynamically.
Conduct A/B testing where one group uses the adaptive kernel and the other uses the baseline fixed precision kernel.
Measure p50/p99 latency and throughput across various workloads and input types to evaluate the effectiveness of adaptive precision.
Analyze profiling data to identify which precision adjustments lead to performance gains and the impact on overall resource utilization.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The adaptive precision mechanism may introduce overhead that could negate its performance benefits.
The need for real-time monitoring may require additional computational resources.
Dynamic adjustments could lead to variability in performance, making it challenging to achieve consistent results.
